text:
I think a lot of people when we try to bring someone to faith, some of the most fundamental
questions that most people at least in the modern era have, concern origin of life when
we're Adam and Eve created, were there other hominids in existence before Adam and Eve were created? Are you willing to speculate on that? Oh, yeah. This is what volume three is all about.
There are extensive discussions in there about paleoanthropology, Neanderthals and Denisovans and they're related. I didn't know that. And I'm thrilled to hear that.
And since I only have volume one, would you help me and my audience by talking a little bit about that? Because this is really fascinating. And I've heard so little on this. Okay.
What I argue at some length is that the genre of Genesis 1 to 11, chapters 1 to 11 are
not straightforward history, but are about historical events that are cloaked in figurative
and metaphorical language and therefore not to be interpreted literally. I think that the scripture does commit us to a historical Adam and Eve who are the progenitors
of the human race and who's fallen to sin, introduced sin into the human race with all of its terrible consequences.
But I do not think that it commits us to saying that this couple lived 10 to 20 thousand years ago. So when I look at the evidence of paleoanthropology, I argue at length that Neanderthals were fully
human. They had modern cognitive capacities just like homo sapiens. And that therefore Adam and Eve as the universal progenitors of humanity must have been ancestral
not only to homo sapiens, but also to Neanderthals. And when you look at the paleoanthropological evidence, that would identify them with what
is called homo-hydobergenzis or hydobergman, who was the last common ancestor of Neanderthals to niece of ants and homo sapiens.
And so my tentative proposal in the book is that Adam and Eve belonged to the species of hydobergman and lived somewhere around 750,000 years ago.
Holy guacamole, as we say, that's extraordinary. I've never heard anything like that and I'm really so fascinated by this and I think most people are.
And I think that as part of what I like to do personally and particularly in Socrates and the city conversations is to speculate, to really wonder what can we know without being afraid to say something.
Now, of course there are people when you say that some of the things we've had said here already, that strike three, they're not going to listen anymore. But I think it really is fascinating and I really believe it's God's will that we do
speculate to the best of our ability without being afraid of getting it wrong. But I've not heard that. I mean, to go back that far is extraordinary. Why would you go back that far?
In other words, why not say a hundred thousand years or eighty thousand years? This is so interesting, Eric.
The reason is that there are what paleoanthropologists call archaeological signatures of modern cognitive
behavior among the antitals and homohydobergenses that go back three hundred fifty thousand years or so ago.
And in the book, I give illustrations like the interesting constructions in Brunei-Cal
Cave in France or the so-called Shernigan spears that were discovered in a coal mine in
Germany or just a couple years ago, a piece of nannter tall manufactured twine where they were actually manufacturing string.
And the archaeologist who excavated said this shows a mathematical ability in these ancient people that is comparable what's required for language.
They had linguistic ability. So we're not talking here about grunting ape men.
We are talking about cognitively modern human persons who have left behind in the archaeological
record these signatures of symbolic and abstract thinking, planning, technical, knowledge, and expertise.
And so that convinces me that we must place Adam and Eve back far enough in history to be ancestral to these persons.
And if I just might say, you said you've not heard this before among other theologians.
And I think the reason for that is that theologians today are not only ignorant of philosophy. They're also terribly ignorant of science.
They don't get schooled in seminary, in physics, or biology, or paleoanthropology.
And so as a result, these theologians may be very good at doing biblical theology, but they're impaired when it comes to doing systematic theology because they don't have the philosophical
and scientific tools to do the job well. Well, of course, I think that's exactly right.
I am myself ignorant enough, I'm not afraid to show my ignorance to ask when roughly does modern science today say Neanderthal lived?
They would be about 350,000 years ago to about 40,000 years ago, and then they went extinct. Homo sapiens continued on to today, and that is the species that you and I are members
of. Though, I love this.
We probably have 5 to 10% of Neanderthal genes in our genome, our DNA, bears the traces
of these ancient Neanderthals who interbred with Homo sapiens, and the same is true of
Denisovans among some South Sea Islanders, Pacific Islanders, up to 5% of their DNA is probably inherited from these Denisovans.
So isn't that incredible that we bear the vestiges of these ancient people that lived so long ago?
Now why would you go so far past Neanderthal? You were talking about Homo Heidelberg and Neensis, I think it sounded sort of something
like that, but Heidelberg man obviously famously found in Heidelberg, Germany, but does Heidelberg man go back as far as 750,000 years ago?
Yes, there are skeletons of him as well, and they predate Neanderthals and are found around 750,000, 500,000 years ago.
So he was the last common ancestor, and so we want to figure out when did he come into being? When did he originate? And this is all very cloudy.
I mean, it can't be pinned down exactly, but these are rough dates. I just think it's fun to speculate. I think God wants us to speculate and to think about these things and to think from what
we know in Scripture and what we know in science, what can we conclude. Do you think that it's possible that, well actually before I ask that, let me just go back.
So I don't believe that we evolved from apes. By going back that far, are you suggesting that you think we might have or do you believe
that we were, I think you've already said that Adam and Eve were specially created by God and that they are the forebears of all of humanity. So you're saying that they were specially--
I do affirm that they are a historical human couple who are the first human beings on this planet, from whom everyone else is descended.
But I do not take a position on their origin, whether God used pre-human hominins to produce
Adam and Eve, I can well imagine where a kind of systemic mutation in such a hominin
would produce the central nervous system and brain capacity to sustain a rational soul.
But I'm a dualist, I believe in soul and body, and so the first human being would have to be the first such creature to have a human soul.
And that requires a certain neurological complexity in order to sustain a rational soul.
But whether or not that had pre-human ancestors, I think, is an open question and a matter of some indifference, honestly. Yeah.
Why would you suppose we don't see civilization "until" you know, whatever, 10,000 years ago, let's say?
Well, I guess it depends on what you mean by civilization area, because as I mentioned
already, these constructions in Bluenekel Cave in France show that these people had the ability to plan and execute construction projects.
And even more recently, since my book, In Quest of the Historical Adam, was published, in Zambia, paleoanthropologist have found evidence of wooden constructions, kind of like
Lincoln logs. You know, they're indented, so they fit together. Yeah. And these things are dated to around 400,000 years ago.
And so they think that these people were constructing wooden structures, maybe platforms or walkways or whatever.
So I think that deserves to be called civilization of a sort. I was going to say, allow me to gas 400,000 years ago.
Now I'm sure there are many people would say that carbon dating is flawed, and we can't know that it's that old. But the very idea that it's possible, I don't see why it's not possible. But I have never heard that.
That is extraordinary. Yeah. The stuff is, it's happening so rapidly, it's just fascinating the finds that they dig up.
If you think these conversations are valuable, would you do us a favor and please like this video and subscribe to this channel. When you do that, you help us get these conversations out to a wider audience by telling YouTube
that people are interested in this. Also, you can go to SocratesInTheCity. com, sign up for our newsletter, and you can check back every week. There's new stuff here every week. Thank you for tuning in.
